author: @Knapweed
text: Although it is a generator in the general sense, it's actually more of a dynamo as it has a commutator and produces direct current. Fascinating video.

author: @HazenMire
text: Smallest motor I've ever seen I pulled out of a cheap electric toothbrush from CVS (American pharmacy/drug store). It had 2 motors in it, one to drive the head of the brush, a 2nd to vibrate the the thing and let you know to move the brush to another quadrant. That motor for vibrating was as tiny as tiny gets. Maybe 1.5-2 mm in diameter and 1cm or so long.

author: @MasterTuttoo
text: so is there a permanent magnet in the motor?

author: @wizrom3046
text:
Nice to see you explaining your electronic test conditions more clearly. 🙂👍
You might like to have a look at VCR capstan motors. They are a low RPM brushless motor with a nice thick shaft. They can easily be hacked by adding a 3 phase rectifier and generate good volts and power at low revs, which is ideal because you don't need much gearing. Tip; the older VCR models had bigger capstan motors and better bearings.

author: @stuartevans6807
text: One idea for a video - the comparison between number of blades on a turbine. Theoretically 1 blade is the most efficient, but more blades with gearing could yeild a better result particularly at low wind speeds, but max output will be lower

author: @karlmyers6518
text: Smart little thing mate

author: @headlessxp4948
text: Wait, wouldnt you want a high voltage low rpm motor?!. If the motor konstant is rpm / U = 2000 kv as an example, you would get 4000 rpm if you apply 2V. But that also means that the output voltage is given as  U = rpm / kv-constant. So you would want the kv constant to be as low as possible to get more voltage at lower rpm.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Sure - but in practice I have found HV High rpm to have low torque - low RPM motors seem to have a much higher torque - so - though amps go up they turn out to be hard to turn - I think it is horses for courses really and actually any motor will do as long as you match the capture device to the motor requirements with gearing - but as a rule of thumb for getting an easy quick result I think what I said helps

replies.author: @headlessxp4948
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Yeah, the low torque part really makes sense now that I think about it

replies.author: @bugz000
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering people get too caught up on one principal, and try to match everything around that one pricipal... which works! and that's why people will argue low rpm motors are better generators.... (it is no judgement headlessXP, your view is not wrong at all! it 100% works :D) but here lies the difference between understanding and comprehension... understanding makes you use lower KV.... comprehension makes you create a well matched system, regardless of motor RPM, which is the true solution!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: @headlessxp4948 I did leave a lot out here - but you have to make a choice in a few minute vid - what you are saying is 100% correct - of course - but a lot of folks just want a result they can 'knock up' in a few hours - if they get success then hopefully they will look further

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: absolutely spot on mate - it's a system and for that has to be in balance
